--- SOURCE 4: Perspectives of CRISPR/Cas-mediated cis-engineering in ... ---
URL: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8

SUMMARY:
Natural genetic changes in cis-regulatory regions of genes play important roles in shaping phenotypic diversity by altering their expression. The advent of CRISPR/Cas-mediated cis-regulatory region engineering (cis-engineering) provides a more refined method for modulating gene expression and creating phenotypic diversity to benefit crop improvement. The genome sequence for at least 181 horticultural species is available11 and genome editing has been used to generate primarily knockout mutations in at least 25 of them12,13,14,15,16,[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR17 "Zhou, J. Natural epigenetic variations contribute to heritable phenotypic diversity that is not caused by modification in the DNA sequence46,47,48,[49](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR49 "Sun, S., Wang, X., Wang, K. Transgene-free genome editing in tomato and potato plants using agrobacterium-mediated delivery of a CRISPR/Cas9 cytidine base editor.

 Perspectives of CRISPR/Cas-mediated _cis_-engineering in horticulture: unlocking the neglected potential for crop improvement 

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8.pdf)

[Download PDF](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8.pdf)

*   Review Article
*   [Open access](https://www.springernature.com/gp/open-research/about/the-fundamentals-of-open-access-and-open-research)
*   Published: 15 March 2020

Perspectives of CRISPR/Cas-mediated _cis_-engineering in horticulture: unlocking the neglected potential for crop improvement
=============================================================================================================================

*   [Qiang Li](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#auth-Qiang-Li-Aff1-Aff2)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#Aff1),[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#Aff2), 
*   [Manoj Sapkota](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#auth-Manoj-Sapkota-Aff3)[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#Aff3)&
*   [Esther van der Knaap](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#auth-Esther-van_der_Knaap-Aff2-Aff3-Aff4)[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#Aff2),[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#Aff3),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#Aff4)

[_Horticulture Research_](https://www.nature.com/hortres)**volume 7**, Article number:36 (2020) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#citeas)

*   10k Accesses

*   75 Altmetric

*   [Metrics details](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Directed breeding of horticultural crops is essential for increasing yield, nutritional content, and consumer-valued characteristics such as shape and color of the produce. However, limited genetic diversity restricts the amount of crop improvement that can be achieved through conventional breeding approaches. Natural genetic changes in _cis_-regulatory regions of genes play important roles in shaping phenotypic diversity by altering their expression. Utilization of CRISPR/Cas editing in crop species can accelerate crop improvement through the introduction of genetic variation in a targeted manner. The advent of CRISPR/Cas-mediated _cis_-regulatory region engineering (_cis_-engineering) provides a more refined method for modulating gene expression and creating phenotypic diversity to benefit crop improvement. Here, we focus on the current applications of CRISPR/Cas-mediated _cis_-engineering in horticultural crops. We describe strategies and limitations for its use in crop improvement, including de novo _cis_-regulatory element (CRE) discovery, precise genome editing, and transgene-free genome editing. In addition, we discuss the challenges and prospects regarding current technologies and achievements. CRISPR/Cas-mediated _cis_-engineering is a critical tool for generating horticultural crops that are better able to adapt to climate change and providing food for an increasing world population.

Article 15 November 2021

Introduction
------------

Horticultural crops comprise vegetables, fruits, and ornamental flowers as well as aromatic and medicinal plants, thereby providing essential resources to society. For example, the availability and consumption of a wide variety of vegetables and fruits allow us to meet our daily dietary needs. Moreover, we enlighten our days with the abundance of floriculture products for aesthetic uses and visual enjoyment. Collectively, horticultural crops make essential contributions to humankind while also providing the economic engines that drive the success of societies all over the world[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR1 "Van den Broeck, G. & Maertens, M. Horticultural exports and food security in developing countries. Glob. Food Secur. 10, 11–20 (2016).").

Despite their collective importance, the improvement of many horticultural crops has lagged behind most agronomic crops, such as rice, corn, and soybean. Yet, improvement of horticultural crops for traits such as resistance to biotic and abiotic stresses, yield, and health-related nutrients would benefit the entire sector. Genetic diversity is a critical source for crop improvement. However, this diversity is often limiting, especially for certain species[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR2 "Shi, J. & Lai, J. Patterns of genomic changes with crop domestication and breeding. Curr. Opin. Plant Biol. 24, 47–53 (2015)."). The limited genetic diversity could result in significant obstacles for further improvement by conventional breeding approaches. Research in several crops has demonstrated that much of the genetic changes underlying traits of economic importance reside in the _cis_-regulatory regions of genes[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR3 "Swinnen, G., Goossens, A. & Pauwels, L. Lessons from domestication: targeting cis-regulatory elements for crop improvement. Trends Plant Sci. 21, 506–515 (2016)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR4 "van der Knaap, E. et al. What lies beyond the eye: the molecular mechanisms regulating tomato fruit weight and shape. Front. Plant Sci. 5, 227 (2014)."). These changes appear to have been selected during domestication, resulting in desirable traits caused by altered gene expression[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR3 "Swinnen, G., Goossens, A. & Pauwels, L. Lessons from domestication: targeting cis-regulatory elements for crop improvement. Trends Plant Sci. 21, 506–515 (2016)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR5 "Meyer, R. S. & Purugganan, M. D. Evolution of crop species: genetics of domestication and diversification. Nat. Rev. Genet. 14, 840–852 (2013)."). The CRISPR/Cas-based platform offers a powerful tool by engineering _cis_-regulatory regions (_cis_-engineering) to introduce genetic diversity that could potentially accelerate crop improvement[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR6 "Li, X., Xie, Y., Zhu, Q. & Liu, Y.-G. Targeted genome editing in genes and cis-regulatory regions improves qualitative and quantitative traits in crops. Mol. Plant 10, 1368–1370 (2017)."),[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR7 "Rodríguez-Leal, D., Lemmon, Z. H., Man, J., Bartlett, M. E. & Lippman, Z. B. Engineering quantitative trait variation for crop improvement by genome editing. Cell 171, 470–480 (2017)."),[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR8 "Birchler, J. A. Editing the phenotype: a revolution for quantitative genetics. Cell 171, 269–270 (2017)."),[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR9 "Wolter, F. & Puchta, H. Application of CRISPR/Cas to Understand Cis- and Trans-Regulatory Elements in Plants. In: Plant Transcription Factors. Methods in Molecular Biology (ed. Yamaguchi, N.) vol 1830. (Humana Press, New York, NY, 2018)."),[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR10 "Wolter, F., Schindele, P. & Puchta, H. Plant breeding at the speed of light: the power of CRISPR/Cas to generate directed genetic diversity at multiple sites. BMC Plant Biol. 19, 176 (2019)."). Despite the importance of regulatory changes in genes, the application of CRISPR/Cas-mediated _cis_-engineering has only been explored sporadically. The genome sequence for at least 181 horticultural species is available[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR11 "Chen, F. et al. Genome sequences of horticultural plants: past, present, and future. Horticult. Res. 6, 1–23 (2019).") and genome editing has been used to generate primarily knockout mutations in at least 25 of them[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR12 "Xu, J., Hua, K. & Lang, Z. Genome editing for horticultural crop improvement. Horticult. Res. 6, 1–16 (2019)."),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR13 "Xiong, J.-S., Ding, J. & Li, Y. Genome-editing technologies and their potential application in horticultural crop breeding. Horticult. Res. 2, 15019 (2015)."),[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR14 "Karkute, S. G., Singh, A. K., Gupta, O. P., Singh, P. M. & Singh, B. CRISPR/Cas9 mediated genome engineering for improvement of horticultural crops. Front. Plant Sci. 8, 1635 (2017)."),[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR15 "Koltun, A., Corte, L. E.-D., Mertz-Henning, L. M. & Gonçalves, L. S. Genetic improvement of horticultural crops mediated by CRISPR/Cas: a new horizon of possibilities. Horticult. Brasil. 36, 290–298 (2018)."),[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR16 "Wang, T., Zhang, H. & Zhu, H. CRISPR technology is revolutionizing the improvement of tomato and other fruit crops. Horticult. Res. 6, 1–13 (2019)."),[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR17 "Zhou, J. et al. Application and future perspective of CRISPR/Cas9 genome editing in fruit crops. J. Integr. Plant Biol. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/jipb.12793
                  
                 (2019)."). These achievements demonstrate the feasibility of applying CRISPR/Cas-mediated _cis_-engineering to expand the phenotypic diversity of many horticultural crops.

Natural variation in _cis_-regulatory regions resulting from the domestication of horticultural crops
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

_Cis_-regulatory regions are non-coding DNA sequences that control the transcription of genes[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR18 "Wittkopp, P. J. & Kalay, G. Cis-regulatory elements: molecular mechanisms and evolutionary processes underlying divergence. Nat. Rev. Genet. 13, 59 (2012)."). These _cis_-regulatory sequences consist of combinations of CREs that affect gene expression level often in a spatiotemporal manner[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR9 "Wolter, F. & Puchta, H. Application of CRISPR/Cas to Understand Cis- and Trans-Regulatory Elements in Plants. In: Plant Transcription Factors. Methods in Molecular Biology (ed. Yamaguchi, N.) vol 1830. (Humana Press, New York, NY, 2018)."),[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR18 "Wittkopp, P. J. & Kalay, G. Cis-regulatory elements: molecular mechanisms and evolutionary processes underlying divergence. Nat. Rev. Genet. 13, 59 (2012)."),[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR19 "Bulger, M. & Groudine, M. Functional and mechanistic diversity of distal transcription enhancers. Cell 144, 327–339 (2011)."). Single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs), insertions, deletions, inversions, and epigenetic variations are the most common natural variation in _cis_-regulatory regions that are associated with domestication. Some examples from horticultural crops are discussed below.

### Single-nucleotide polymorphisms

Genomic studies in horticultural crops have generated insights into the role of SNP in shaping phenotypic diversity among individuals[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR20 "Huq, M. A. et al. Identification of functional SNPs in genes and their effects on plant phenotypes. J. Plant Biotechnol. 43, 1–11 (2016)."). During tomato (_Solanum lycopersicum_) domestication, selection frequently occurred for fruit size and shape, traits that show extensive variation and large increases over that of the wild relatives[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR4 "van der Knaap, E. et al. What lies beyond the eye: the molecular mechanisms regulating tomato fruit weight and shape. Front. Plant Sci. 5, 227 (2014)."). Increases in fruit weight are thought to be controlled by SNPs in the promoter of _FW2.2_ (_SlCNR_) and _FW3.2_ (_SlKLUH_)[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR4 "van der Knaap, E. et al. What lies beyond the eye: the molecular mechanisms regulating tomato fruit weight and shape. Front. Plant Sci. 5, 227 (2014)."),[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR21 "Frary, A. et al. fw2. 2: a quantitative trait locus key to the evolution of tomato fruit size. Science 289, 85–88 (2000)."),[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR22 "Chakrabarti, M. et al. A cytochrome P450 regulates a domestication trait in cultivated tomato. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. 110, 17125-17130 (2013)."). The _lc_ allele contains two SNPs in a 15-bp repressor element downstream of tomato _WUSCHEL_ (_SlWUS_). The SNPs are proposed to prevent the binding of the MADS-box transcription factor AGAMOUS, which is required to recruit the repressive Polycomb proteins to shut down _SlWUS_ expression, thereby ultimately resulting in larger fruits[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR4 "van der Knaap, E. et al. What lies beyond the eye: the molecular mechanisms regulating tomato fruit weight and shape. Front. Plant Sci. 5, 227 (2014)."),[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR23 "Muños, S. et al. Increase in tomato locule number is controlled by two single-nucleotide polymorphisms located near WUSCHEL. Plant Physiol. 156, 2244–2254 (2011)."),[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR24 "Chu, Y. H., Jang, J. C., Huang, Z. & van der Knaap, E. Tomato locule number and fruit size controlled by natural alleles of lc and fas. Plant Direct 3, e00142 (2019)."). In another example in tomato, two SNPs in the promoter of _Slcyc-B_ are highly associated with high β-carotene content[25](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR25 "Orchard, C. Naturally Occurring Variation in the Promoter of the Chromoplast-Specific Cyc-B Gene in Tomato can be used to Modulate Levels of ß-Carotene in Ripe Tomato Fruit (The Ohio State University, 2014).").

In citrus (_Citrus reticulata_), a recent report found an SNP in a miniature inverted-repeat transposable element (MITE) in the promoter of _carotenoid cleavage dioxygenase 4b_ (_CCD4_) to be sufficient to increase the expression of this gene, resulting in red coloration of fruit peel[26](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR26 "Zheng, X. et al. Natural variation in CCD4 promoter underpins species-specific evolution of red coloration in citrus peel. Mol. Plant 12, 1294–1307 (2019)."). In pepper (_Capsicum chinense_), an SNP in the promoter of _MYB31_ is associated with a hyperfunctional W-box, leading to stronger binding of WRKY9. This stronger binding is associated with enhanced expression of _MYB31_, resulting in extremely pungent peppers[27](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR27 "Zhu, Z. et al. Natural variations in the MYB transcription factor MYB31 determine the evolution of extremely pungent peppers. N. Phytol. 223, 922–938 (2019).").

### Insertions

Insertions are sources of genetic diversity that can alter gene expression by introducing new or disrupting existing CREs. Especially transposable elements (TEs) play important roles in creating genomic variation by altering gene regulation[28](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR28 "Lisch, D. How important are transposons for plant evolution? Nat. Rev. Genet. 14, 49 (2013)."),[29](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR29 "Olsen, K. M. & Wendel, J. F. A bountiful harvest: genomic insights into crop domestication phenotypes. Annu. Rev. Plant Biol. 64, 47–70 (2013)."). TE-induced variations in _cis_-regulatory region are also important in the shaping of domestication-related phenotypes in many horticultural crops. One example is the tomato fruit shape gene _SUN_. The transposition event at the _sun_ locus mediated by the _Rider_ retrotransposon placed a copy of _SUN_ in addition to _Rider_ itself in the intron of _DEFL1_. The ancestral copy of _SUN_ on chromosome 10 is lowly expressed, but its derived copy on chromosome 7, where the _sun_ locus maps, is highly expressed[30](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR30 "Xiao, H., Jiang, N., Schaffner, E., Stockinger, E. J. & Van Der Knaap, E. A retrotransposon-mediated gene duplication underlies morphological variation of tomato fruit. Science 319, 1527–1530 (2008)."). The high expression of _SUN_ on chromosome 7 is thought to be from the promoter of _DEFL1_ that would now serve as an enhancer of _SUN_, leading to the elongated tomato fruit[31](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR31 "Jiang, N., Gao, D., Xiao, H. & Van Der Knaap, E. Genome organization of the tomato sun locus and characterization of the unusual retrotransposon Rider. Plant J. 60, 181–193 (2009)."). Another _Rider_ insertion in the first intron of _SEPALLATA4_ (_SEP4_) leads to a jointless pedicel, reduced fruit dropping, which facilitates mechanical harvesting[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR32 "Soyk, S. et al. Bypassing negative epistasis on yield in tomato imposed by a domestication gene. Cell 169, 1142–1155 (2017)."). In grape (_Vitis vinifera_), the insertion of the _Gret1_ (Grapevine Retrotransposon 1) in the _VvMYBA1_ promoter leads to its inactivation, resulting in a white berry phenotype[33](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR33 "Kobayashi, S., Goto-Yamamoto, N. & Hirochika, H. Retrotransposon-induced mutations in grape skin color. Science 304, 982–982 (2004)."). In blood oranges (_Citrus sinensis_), the insertion of a _Copia_-like retrotransposon controls the expression of _Ruby_ and the cold dependency of anthocyanin production in the fruit[34](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR34 "Butelli, E. et al. Retrotransposons control fruit-specific, cold-dependent accumulation of anthocyanins in blood oranges. Plant Cell 24, 1242–1255 (2012)."). In cauliflower (_Brassica oleracea_ var _botrytis_), a 695-bp _Harbinger_ DNA transposon insertion in the _MYB2_ promoter increases the expression of this gene, resulting in a purple phenotype[35](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR35 "Chiu, L.-W. et al. The purple cauliflower arises from activation of a MYB transcription factor. Plant Physiol. 154, 1470–1480 (2010)."). Additionally, the differentiation of winter and spring genotypes in rapeseed (_Brassica napus_ L.) primarily arose from a MITE transposon insertion in the upstream region of _BnFLC.A10_[36](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR36 "Hou, J. et al. A Tourist-like MITE insertion in the upstream region of the BnFLC. A10 gene is associated with vernalization requirement in rapeseed (Brassica napus L.). BMC Plant Biol. 12, 238 (2012).").

Other examples of insertions that are possibly associated with TE activity are found as well. In tomato, _ej2_ _w_ (_enhancer-of-jointless 2_) is a weak loss-of-function allele, which was selected during tomato domestication and caused by a 564-bp insertion in the fifth intron of _EJ2_. The mutation results in unbranched inflorescences with exceptionally large sepals[32](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR32 "Soyk, S. et al. Bypassing negative epistasis on yield in tomato imposed by a domestication gene. Cell 169, 1142–1155 (2017)."). An 8-bp insertion in the promoter of _SlbHLH59_ significantly increased its expression in accessions producing high ascorbic acid levels[37](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR37 "Ye, J. et al. Genome-wide association analysis identifies a natural variation in basic helix-loop-helix transcription factor regulating ascorbate biosynthesis via d-mannose/l-galactose pathway in tomato. PLoS Genet. 15, e1008149 (2019)."). In apple (_Malus_ × _domestica_), multiple repeats of a 23-bp motif in the promoter of _MYB10_ generate an autoregulatory locus, which is sufficient to account for increased expression and ectopic accumulation of anthocyanins in red-fleshed apples[38](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR38 "Espley, R. V. et al. Multiple repeats of a promoter segment causes transcription factor autoregulation in red apples. Plant Cell 21, 168–183 (2009)."). Another example from apple is that a 36-bp insertion in _MdSAUR37_ promoter contributed to high fruit malate content[39](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR39 "Jia, D. et al. Apple fruit acidity is genetically diversified by natural variations in three hierarchical epistatic genes: MdSAUR37, MdPP2CH and MdALMTII. Plant J. 95, 427–443 (2018)."). In cucumber (_Cucumis sativus_ L.), a 10-bp fragment was replaced by an 812-bp fragment in the promoter of _CsHDZIV11_/_CsGL3_ at the _few spines 1_ (_fs1_) locus, giving rise to higher expression of _CsGL3_ and fewer fruit spines[40](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR40 "Zhang, H. et al. A fragment substitution in the promoter of CsHDZIV11/CsGL3 is responsible for fruit spine density in cucumber (Cucumis sativus L.). Theor. Appl. Genet. 129, 1289–1301 (2016).").

### Deletions and inversions

Deletions are common genetic changes that provide a wealth of domesticated related phenotypic diversity. One remarkable example is a 31-kb deletion upstream of tomato _OVATE Family Protein 20_ (_SlOFP20_). The deletion is associated with reduced expression of _SlOFP20_ and contributes to natural fruit shape variation in the tomato germplasm[41](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR41 "Wu, S. et al. A common genetic mechanism underlies morphological diversity in fruits and other plant organs. Nat. Commun. 9, 4734 (2018)."). A 3-bp deletion in the promoter of tomato _Al-ACTIVATED MALATE TRANSPORTER9_ (_Sl-ALMT9_) was selected during tomato domestication. The deletion disrupts the repression of _Sl-ALMT9_ by Sl-WRKY42. This results in increased _Sl-ALMT9_ gene expression levels, thereby conferring high fruit malate contents and aluminum tolerance in tomato[42](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR42 "Ye, J. et al. An InDel in the promoter of Al-ACTIVATED MALATE TRANSPORTER9 selected during tomato domestication determines fruit malate contents and aluminum tolerance. Plant Cell 29, 2249–2268 (2017)."). Flowering time is an important trait for cucumber domestication. A 39.9-kb deletion and a 16.2-kb deletion located 16.5-kb upstream of cucumber _FLOWERING LOCUS T_ (_CsFT_) are both associated with higher _CsFT_ expression levels and earlier flowering[43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR43 "Wang, S. et al. FLOWERING LOCUS T improved cucumber adaptation to higher latitudes. Plant Physiol. 182, 908–918 (2020)."). The _CsFT_ locus was selected during cucumber domestication and has been important in its adaptation to higher latitudes for cultivation[43](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR43 "Wang, S. et al. FLOWERING LOCUS T improved cucumber adaptation to higher latitudes. Plant Physiol. 182, 908–918 (2020)."). Therefore, deletions can confer desirable traits through either decreased gene expression by removing enhancers and binding sites of activators or increased gene expression by removing repressors and binding sites of repressors.

Genomic inversions also play a role in plant domestication as they could have widespread _cis_-regulatory effects[44](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR44 "Said, I. et al. Linked genetic variation and not genome structure causes widespread differential expression associated with chromosomal inversions. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 115, 5492–5497 (2018)."). One of the remarkable examples of variation in locule number is controlled by a nearly 300-kb inversion of the _fasciated_ (_fas_) locus in tomato. The _fas_ locus is characterized by disruption of the promoter region of tomato _CLAVATA3_ (_SlCLV3_), leading to downregulation of the gene and larger fruit with increased number of locules[24](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR24 "Chu, Y. H., Jang, J. C., Huang, Z. & van der Knaap, E. Tomato locule number and fruit size controlled by natural alleles of lc and fas. Plant Direct 3, e00142 (2019)."),[45](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR45 "Xu, C. et al. A cascade of arabinosyltransferases controls shoot meristem size in tomato. Nat. Genet. 47, 784 (2015).").

### Epigenetic variations

Natural epigenetic variations contribute to heritable phenotypic diversity that is not caused by modification in the DNA sequence[46](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR46 "Gallusci, P. et al. Epigenetics for plant improvement: current knowledge and modeling avenues. Trends Plant Sci. 22, 610–623 (2017)."),[47](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR47 "Arencibia, A. D., D’Afonseca, V., Chakravarthi, M. & Castiglione, S. Learning from transgenics: advanced gene editing technologies should also bridge the gap with traditional genetic selection. Electron. J. Biotechnol. 41, 22–29 (2019)."),[48](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41438-020-0258-8#ref-CR48 "Kumar, S. Epigenomics for crop improvement: current status and future

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

by H Saini · 2023 · Cited by 38 — CRISPR/Cas9-based genome editing is a fundamental breakthrough technique to accelerate plant breeding and crop improvement program of various crops.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Statistical analysis involves evaluating CRISPR editing outcomes through quantitative metrics, ensuring results are valid and reproducible, as highlighted in horticultural crop studies.
Comparative studies assess CRISPR-edited plants against non-edited controls, elucidating phenotypic differences and validating the efficacy of genome editing across various horticultural species.
Impact assessments evaluate the ecological and economic consequences of CRISPR modifications, guiding responsible implementation in horticulture, ensuring sustainable practices and crop improvement.
